evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~14 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Human voice vs committee 2/10 — Reads as brand editorial copy with no individual voice; hedged, plural, and formulaic throughout.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — Has two genuinely opinionated lines (Ramadan/theatre, mental-health framing), but much of it is hedged safe advice.
Unexpected phrasings 5/10 — The 78-day/iftar line and '45°C August' are fresh, but most copy is predictable wellness phrasing.
Specific citations 6/10 — Has some specifics (Ramadan date, AED 1,200, age 40) but references are vague ('BMJ', 'The Lancet') without titles, authors, or years.
Point of view 3/10 — The piece surveys guidelines rather than taking a position; it never says anyone is wrong about anything.
Quotable sentence 6/10 — The '78 days to first iftar' line and 'fewer March collapses, fewer guilty July restarts' are quotable; rest is forgettable.
Intro earns attention 5/10 — Opening is generic; the strong '78 days' hook is buried three paragraphs down.

specificity 6/10 — Several passages float in abstraction and motivational language without anchoring claims in numbers, named sources, or concrete examples. 'Quarterly reviews promote ongoing adjustment, not rigid self-judgment' and 'Long-term adherence grows from small, localised course corrections, not perfection' are representative offenders — they make no reference to a specific study finding, statistic, or named mechanism. The BMJ and Lancet are cited by name but no finding (odds ratio, percentage, sample size) is ever quoted, leaving the specificity largely gestural rather than evidential.
